skill.name: linkerd-patterns
skill.description: Apply Linkerd service mesh patterns for Kubernetes installations, traffic policy, mTLS, observability, and multi-cluster deployments.
skill.summary: Build and operate Linkerd service mesh patterns for Kubernetes workloads.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged shell, network, and weak-crypto patterns in SKILL.md. Review found these are documentation examples for Linkerd and Kubernetes, with one real operational risk from a curl-to-shell installer and multiple cluster-mutating commands.

medium_findings.title: Installer Piped Directly To Shell
medium_findings.description: SKILL.md documents downloading the Linkerd installer from a URL and piping it directly to sh. This is a real supply-chain risk if copied without verification, although it appears in a legitimate installation guide and not in executable skill code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pipe-to-shell command is explicit on line 58. Context shows documentation rather than hidden execution, so the risk is operational guidance, not confirmed malware.

medium_findings.title: Cluster-Mutating Command Examples
medium_findings.description: The skill includes Linkerd and kubectl commands that install CRDs, install the control plane, apply multi-cluster resources, and label services. These examples are expected for the skill purpose but can change live Kubernetes clusters if run without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly shown in bash blocks and perform Kubernetes changes. Their legitimate Linkerd context lowers concern about malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Static Pattern False Positives In Documentation
low_findings.description: Several external command detections occur on Markdown code-fence boundaries or YAML examples, and the weak-crypto detections are words such as security-focused and identity. The hardcoded IP finding is a private CIDR in an authorization policy example.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line review shows these signals are prose, diagrams, or Kubernetes YAML examples. They do not contain executable skill logic or evidence of secret exfiltration.

low_findings.title: External Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The skill links to Linkerd documentation and an example API server address. These network references support user education and do not transmit local data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation or placeholder endpoint references. No code sends credentials, files, or environment data to those URLs.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe To Shell Installation Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented installer uses a network download piped to sh. Users should verify installer contents and prefer pinned, reviewed installation methods when possible.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: This exact pattern is present in the installation template. It is dangerous if copied blindly, even though it is not automatically executed by the skill.

content.value_statement: Kubernetes teams need repeatable Linkerd patterns for mTLS, routing, retries, and observability. This skill provides focused templates and commands for common Linkerd service mesh tasks.
actual_capabilities: Provides Linkerd installation and verification command sequences.
Shows namespace and deployment injection annotations.
Defines ServiceProfile examples with routes, retries, timeouts, and retry budgets.
Provides TrafficSplit, HTTPRoute, Server, and ServerAuthorization YAML examples.
Lists Linkerd viz monitoring and debugging commands.
Shows multi-cluster install, link, export, and verification commands.
limitations: Does not validate YAML against a live Kubernetes cluster.
Does not generate environment-specific certificates, identities, or RBAC rules.
Uses example service names, namespaces, ports, and addresses that require customization.
Includes operational commands that should be reviewed before use on production clusters.

use_cases.title: Install a Lightweight Service Mesh
use_cases.description: Use the installation and check sequence to plan a Linkerd control plane rollout.

use_cases.title: Create Safer Service Policies
use_cases.description: Use Server and ServerAuthorization examples to scope service access with mesh identities.

use_cases.title: Operate Canary Deployments
use_cases.description: Use TrafficSplit and HTTPRoute patterns to describe gradual traffic migration between service versions.

prompt_templates.title: Start With Linkerd Install
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to outline the Linkerd install and verification steps for a Kubernetes cluster. Include safety checks before applying changes.

prompt_templates.title: Add Mesh Injection
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Linkerd injection plan for namespace my-app and deployment api. Explain which annotations must be customized.

prompt_templates.title: Design Traffic Policy
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft a ServiceProfile for a user API with retryable GET routes, non-retryable POST routes, timeouts, and a retry budget.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Multi-Cluster Routing
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to plan Linkerd multi-cluster setup for west and east clusters. Include service export and verification steps.

output_examples.input: Plan Linkerd onboarding for a new service.
output_examples.output: A concise rollout plan covering preflight checks, namespace injection, service profile creation, policy review, and post-install verification.
A TrafficSplit-oriented deployment approach that starts with low canary weight, verifies metrics, and increases traffic after review.
A debugging checklist using proxy checks, route metrics, stat views, service dependency views, and proxy log inspection.

best_practices: Run Linkerd preflight and health checks before and after cluster changes.
Customize namespaces, service accounts, ports, routes, and retry budgets for each workload.
Review commands that install resources or change traffic before running them on production clusters.
anti_patterns: Copying example commands into a production cluster without reviewing context.
Allowing unauthenticated traffic without limiting networks and documenting the reason.
Enabling retries without route-level idempotency checks and retry budgets.

faq.question: Does this skill install Linkerd automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides commands and YAML examples that a user must review and run separately.

faq.question: Can it help with automatic mTLS?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains Linkerd mesh injection and includes policy examples that rely on mesh identities.

faq.question: Does it include canary deployment guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a TrafficSplit example with stable and canary backend weights.

faq.question: Can it generate service profiles?
faq.answer: It provides a ServiceProfile template with route matching, failure classification, timeouts, retries, and retry budgets.

faq.question: Is multi-cluster support covered?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes commands for multicluster install, cluster linking, service export, and verification.

faq.question: What should users review before running commands?
faq.answer: Users should review cluster context, namespaces, service names, API addresses, downloaded installers, and kubectl apply operations.
